FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study cohort, US-based, N=6469 children from 21 recruitment sites across US

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media (time on SM, measured via Youth Screen Time Survey), video games, television/videos as separate variables; each with individual regression coefficients reported. Applies B1: individual DM usage with separate β reported

FT_IC3.evidence: Quadratic mixed-effects modelling with regression coefficients (β) reported; e.g. social media usage × time interaction with cerebellum volume: β = 0.02, p < 0.001 (Table 2)

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric β coefficients and p-values throughout Table 2; e.g. 'Social media usage: β = 0.01, p < 0.001'; 95% CI provided in text and supplementary materials

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD data; longitudinal prospective design with quadratic mixed-effects modelling examining SM → brain development relationships

FT_IC6.evidence: Published 2024 in Scientific Reports; no indication of preprint/abstract/deposit duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Fails condition (a): SM is not the DV in all analyses. Multiple SM → brain outcome analyses (e.g., social media usage predicting cerebellum volumes). This is SM-as-IV → outcome, not SM-as-DV. Additionally, fails condition (b): includes cogPGS (polygenic score) as predictor, which is explicitly disqualifying per B17 (genetic/polygenic scores not in demographic-only set). Also includes SES, age, sex as predictors but primary predictors are DM usage variables, not demographics alone.
notes: High social media usage associated with reduced cerebellum development trajectory. Study finds SM effects on brain structure are significant and persistent across sensitivity analyses.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study cohort, US-based, N=10,410 eligible participants from multiple US sites

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying victimization is the ONLY measured outcome. No separately-measured smartphone or social media variable reported independently. Family environment (parental monitoring, family conflict) are predictors; cyberbullying is outcome. Applies B3: cyberbullying alone without separately-measured SM with own coefficient → NOT_MET

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails at FT_IC2; FT_EC7 not evaluated
notes: Paper examines family environment → cyberbullying victimization; cyberbullying is treated as outcome. No independent SM or smartphone exposure variable measured. Keywords mention 'adolescent screen use' but this is not operationalized as a measured variable in the study design.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study data, US-based cohort, uses first five waves (Waves 1, 3, 5 for dependent variables; Waves 3, 4, 5 for mediators)

FT_IC2.evidence: No smartphone or social media variable present. Study examines traumatic stress exposure → impulse control/sensation-seeking, with deviant peer association as mediator. Deviant peer association measured via Peer Behavior Profile (antisocial behaviors, school suspension, fighting); no SM/smartphone qualifying exposure

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails at FT_IC2; FT_EC7 not evaluated
notes: Study uses ABCD data to examine dual systems model development (impulse control, sensation-seeking) as function of traumatic stress and deviant peer association. No smartphone or social media variables present. Structural equation modeling used; mediation pathways tested (indirect effects reported), but no SM/smartphone exposure variable to meet FT_IC2 inclusion.
